splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on when actuarial assumptions used to calculate employer withdrawal liability from multiemployer pension plans must be finalized. This affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in such plans, as the timing directly controls how much withdrawing employers must pay.

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that the plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions after the end of the prior plan year, allowing the use of updated information in calculating withdrawal liability.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions must be fixed by the statutory measurement date (end of the prior plan year), providing employers with greater predictability about potential liability.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending the court-ordered period beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. The outcome affects revocation hearings for thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year.

positions.label: Tolling applies
positions.summary: Holds that the term of supervised release is tolled when a supervisee absconds, so the supervised release period does not run during the time the person is a fugitive.

positions.label: Tolling does not apply
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory authority for judicially-created fugitive tolling of supervised release, so the term continues to run even when the supervisee absconds.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: Circuits are split 6-to-3 on whether federal prisoners face the additional gatekeeping requirement of § 2244(b)(1)—which categorically bars claims previously presented—when bringing successive collateral attacks under § 2255. If it applies, federal prisoners are barred from re-raising any prior claim; if not, they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new constitutional rule.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that the categorical bar in § 2244(b)(1) on relitigating previously raised claims applies to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions, imposing a stricter gatekeeping requirement.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1)'s categorical bar does not apply to federal prisoners under § 2255, so they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s own requirements for successive motions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates have standing to bring pre-election challenges to voting procedures such as ballot-counting deadlines. The resolution affects the ability to challenge election administration practices nationwide before an election takes place, with implications for mail-in voting rules across states.

positions.label: Candidate has standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates for federal office suffer a cognizable competitive injury sufficient for Article III standing when challenging state election rules that allegedly dilute lawful votes.

positions.label: Candidate lacks standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate's generalized grievance about state election rules does not constitute a concrete, particularized injury sufficient for Article III standing.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity trumps deference to agency interpretations (Auer/Kisor deference) when construing ambiguous provisions in the Sentencing Guidelines that increase criminal penalties.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the priority between the rule of lenity and agency or Sentencing Commission deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions. The Third Circuit held that deference trumps lenity, while the Fourth, Fifth, and Eleventh Circuits hold that lenity prevails over deference in criminal contexts. The split has deepened after Loper Bright overruled Chevron.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity, as an ancient canon of construction, takes priority over agency or Sentencing Commission deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions, preventing the government from expanding criminal liability through deference.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that Auer/Kisor deference to the Sentencing Commission's commentary takes priority over the rule of lenity when interpreting ambiguous Guidelines provisions, even in criminal contexts.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (any one criterion is disqualifying).
splits.description: A deep circuit split exists on whether § 3553(f)(1)'s use of 'and' to connect three criminal-history disqualifying criteria means a defendant must meet all three to be disqualified (conjunctive) or any one (disjunctive). This determines whether defendants are sentenced below statutory minimums, with identical criminal histories producing vastly different sentences depending on the circuit.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required
positions.summary: Holds that 'and' in § 3553(f)(1) is unambiguously conjunctive, so a defendant is disqualified from safety-valve relief only if all three criminal-history criteria are met.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that 'and' in § 3553(f)(1) should be read disjunctively, so possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria disqualifies a defendant from safety-valve relief.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which expanded the definition of 'controlled substance offense' to include conspiracy and other inchoate offenses, is entitled to deference under Stinson/Kisor.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Sentencing Commission's commentary adding inchoate offenses (conspiracy, aiding and abetting, attempt) to the career-offender guideline's definition of 'controlled substance offense' is a valid, deferrable interpretation of the guideline text, which does not mention inchoate offenses. This affects whether defendants with prior drug conspiracy convictions are subject to significantly enhanced sentences.

positions.label: Commentary is entitled to deference
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is a reasonable interpretation of an ambiguous guideline and is entitled to controlling deference, so inchoate offenses like conspiracy qualify as 'controlled substance offenses' under the career-offender guideline.

positions.label: Commentary exceeds guideline text
positions.summary: Holds that the guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses and the commentary impermissibly expands the definition, so it is not entitled to deference.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), may be considered as an extraordinary and compelling reason for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply divided on whether courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law—particularly the First Step Act's elimination of mandatory consecutive 'stacked' § 924(c) sentences—as a factor supporting compassionate release. Some circuits categorically bar consideration of such changes, while others permit them alone or in combination with other factors. This affects thousands of inmates serving lengthy stacked sentences.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in sentencing law may be considered, alone or in combination with other factors, as an extraordinary and compelling reason for compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot be considered
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in sentencing law cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release, whether offered alone or in combination with other factors.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state schedule criminalizes substances not listed on the federal Controlled Substances Act schedules.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in § 4B1.2(b) is limited to substances controlled under federal law or also encompasses substances controlled only under state law. This creates sentencing disparities for defendants with prior state drug convictions involving substances like certain marijuana derivatives or analogs not federally scheduled.

positions.label: State-only substances qualify
positions.summary: Holds that § 4B1.2(b) broadly defines 'controlled substance offense' to include offenses under state law, even if the particular substance is not controlled under federal law, because there is no textual basis to graft a federal limitation.

positions.label: Only federally scheduled substances qualify
positions.summary: Holds that the Guidelines' 'controlled substance offense' definition requires the substance to be one controlled under federal law, applying the categorical approach to match state offenses against federal drug schedules.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of a debtor's asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor upon good-faith conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value between the filing of a Chapter 13 petition and conversion to Chapter 7 inures to the bankruptcy estate or remains with the debtor. The Ninth Circuit held that such appreciation belongs to the estate, creating a split with circuits that protect debtors' post-petition appreciation upon good-faith conversion.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that post-petition, pre-conversion increases in equity are 'property of the estate' under § 348(f)(1)(A) because § 541(a) broadly encompasses all interests including appreciation, meaning the trustee can recover the increased value for creditors.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that § 348(f)(1)(A) was designed to protect good-faith converting debtors by limiting estate property to its value as of the original petition date, so post-petition appreciation remains with the debtor.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may expressly consider punishment as a purpose when revoking supervised release and imposing a sentence upon revocation.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether punishment is a permissible sentencing purpose at supervised release revocation proceedings. The Sixth Circuit, under United States v. Lewis, permits courts to punish defendants for violations of supervised release, while other circuits limit the purposes of revocation sentences to deterrence and protection of the public, excluding retributive punishment.

positions.label: Punishment is permissible
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly impose punishment as a purpose when sentencing defendants upon revocation of supervised release.

positions.label: Punishment is not permissible
positions.summary: Holds that the primary purposes of a revocation sentence are to sanction the breach of trust and promote deterrence and public protection, not to punish the defendant for the underlying violation conduct.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the appeal deadline for a Hyde Amendment attorney's fees order is governed by the civil timeline (60 days when the government is a party) or the criminal timeline (14 days).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees, which arises in a criminal case, is a civil matter for purposes of the appellate filing deadline. The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies because the motion is ancillary to the criminal case, while at least one circuit applies the criminal deadline.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the 60-day civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: United States v. Manchester Farming P'ship

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that because the Hyde Amendment motion arises in a criminal case, the 14-day criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b) governs.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal prisoners challenging the conditions or terms of their confinement (as opposed to the fact or duration) must proceed via habeas corpus or may bring civil rights claims under Bivens or other statutes.
splits.description: A longstanding circuit split exists over whether habeas corpus is the exclusive remedy for federal prisoners challenging conditions-of-confinement decisions by the BOP, or whether such challenges may also proceed as civil rights actions. The split affects prisoners' ability to challenge BOP decisions on matters like earned time credits, facility transfers, and placement in prerelease custody.

positions.label: Habeas is exclusive for duration challenges
positions.summary: Holds that challenges to BOP decisions affecting the duration or execution of a sentence—including prerelease custody timing—must be brought as habeas petitions under § 2241, not as civil rights actions.

positions.label: Civil rights claims also available
positions.summary: Holds that prisoners may bring civil rights claims (e.g., under Bivens or § 1983) to challenge conditions or execution of confinement when the relief sought would not necessarily result in earlier release.

circuits.name: Third Circuit
circuits.caseName: Woodall v. Federal Bureau of Prisons

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of discrimination in the operation of hospitality or business establishments under 42 U.S.C. § 1981 or analogous federal civil rights statutes?
splits.description: The Supreme Court granted certiorari to review the Second Circuit's approach to discrimination claims in hospitality settings, indicating a circuit split on the applicable legal standards for establishing liability and available remedies. The decision may resolve disagreements on how business operators must treat customers and what damages are recoverable.

positions.label: Second Circuit's approach
positions.summary: Applied particular legal standards for evaluating discrimination claims against hospitality businesses that the Supreme Court found sufficiently unsettled to warrant review.

positions.label: Other circuits' approach
positions.summary: Applied different legal standards or frameworks for evaluating similar discrimination claims against business establishments, creating the split that prompted certiorari.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the FAAAA's broad preemption clause—which bars states from enacting or enforcing laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any... broker'—preempts common-law negligence claims against freight brokers for negligently selecting or hiring motor carriers. This affects the ability of accident victims to sue intermediaries in the trucking industry.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are preempted by the FAAAA because broker selection of carriers relates to the 'service' of a broker within the meaning of the statute.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that traditional state-law negligence claims against freight brokers for negligent hiring or selection of carriers are not preempted because they do not relate to a broker's 'service' in the statutory sense.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether compassionate release motions may be used to address claims that overlap with habeas corpus territory—such as alleged legal errors in the original conviction or sentence—as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis. Some circuits permit consideration of such factors while others hold they must be raised exclusively through habeas proceedings.

positions.label: May consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that courts may consider claims related to potential errors or unfairness in a conviction or sentence as factors supporting extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release.

positions.label: Cannot use compassionate release for habeas-type claims
positions.summary: Holds that compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A) is not a vehicle for collateral attacks on the validity of a conviction or sentence, and such claims must be pursued through habeas proceedings.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether FIFRA's preemption provision bars state tort claims that effectively require pesticide manufacturers to add warnings beyond what the EPA-approved label includes. This split affects product liability litigation involving widely used agricultural chemicals including Roundup.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims because such claims impose labeling requirements 'in addition to or different from' those under FIFRA.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law failure-to-warn claims are not preempted by FIFRA because they run parallel to federal requirements and do not impose additional labeling obligations.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on how broadly to define the class of 'transportation workers' exempt from the FAA under Section 1, particularly whether last-mile delivery workers who transport goods across state lines or distribute goods that have traveled in interstate commerce qualify for the exemption.

positions.label: Broad reading — delivery workers qualify
positions.summary: Holds that workers who deliver goods that have moved in interstate commerce, including last-mile distributors of baked goods, are transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce exempt from the FAA.

positions.label: Narrow reading — delivery workers do not qualify
positions.summary: Holds that last-mile delivery workers are not 'transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the FAA because they do not work in the transportation industry.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Religious Land Use and Institutionalized Persons Act (RLUIPA) authorizes individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether RLUIPA creates a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials in their individual capacities. Some circuits have held that RLUIPA, enacted under the Spending Clause, does not clearly authorize personal-liability damages against individuals who are not direct recipients of federal funds, while others have allowed such claims to proceed.

positions.label: RLUIPA authorizes individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA provides a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials sued in their individual capacities for violations of prisoners' religious exercise rights.

positions.label: RLUIPA does not authorize individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA does not clearly authorize individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials because Congress cannot impose personal liability on non-recipient individuals through the Spending Clause.

circuits.name: Fifth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Sossamon v. Lone Star State of Texas

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Landor v. Louisiana Department of Corrections and Public Safety

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the federal law prohibiting unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Bruen establishing a historical-tradition test for Second Amendment challenges, the circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances (particularly marijuana) is constitutional. This implicates the intersection of expanding state marijuana legalization with federal firearms law.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) does not violate the Second Amendment because historical tradition supports disarming individuals whose drug use renders them dangerous or untrustworthy with firearms.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical tradition of disarming persons for drug use alone.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment
